one_line_takeaway: AgentHazard shows that harmful behavior in computer-use agents frequently emerges only from the composition of locally-benign multi-step actions, and that neither backbone-model alignment (ASR up to 82.90% for GLM-4.6/Claude Code) nor standalone text-based guard models (best unsafe-detection rate only 27.03% at full trajectory) reliably catch it.
key_finding: Current computer-use agents are broadly vulnerable to trajectory-composed harm and alignment does not transfer from model to agent level. The single highest reported ASR is GLM-4.6 under Claude Code at 82.90% (avg. harmfulness score 7.05/10); Qwen3-Coder under Claude Code reaches 73.63% (score 5.95), the figure highlighted in the abstract. OpenClaw's peak is Kimi-K2 at 71.10% (score 5.82), and IFlow with Qwen2.5-Coder-32B-Instruct reaches 74.70% (score 6.06). The same backbone shows large framework-dependent spread: Qwen2.5-Coder-32B-Instruct scores 57.80% ASR under Claude Code vs. 64.06% under OpenClaw vs. 74.70% under IFlow (over 16 points of variance attributed to system prompts, tool routing, and permission boundaries rather than the model itself). Harm escalates sharply with more accumulated steps: for Qwen2.5-Coder-32B-Instruct, ASR rises from round_1 to round_all as 33.50%->43.00% (Claude Code), 23.46%->64.21% (IFlow), and 29.93%->64.10% (OpenClaw), with IFlow and OpenClaw roughly tripling ASR by round_3. Standalone guard models are weak pre-execution filters: at round_1 no guard model exceeds 5% unsafe-detection rate; at round_all the best (Llama-Guard-3-8B) reaches only 27.03%, while the three Qwen3Guard variants plateau at 15.30-16.59% regardless of parameter count (0.6B to 8B), which the authors interpret as a training-distribution bottleneck rather than a capacity bottleneck. Attack-strategy analysis: OpenClaw shows a pronounced spike at Dependency Hook Trigger (70.43% average ASR), while Implicit Indirect Injection is the weakest strategy in both Claude Code and OpenClaw; Persistence Establishment and Resource Exhaustion risk categories consistently yield higher ASRs across models/frameworks, while Prompt Intelligence Theft tends to show lower ASR.
core_contribution: The paper introduces AgentHazard, a benchmark of 2,653 curated instances (seed pool 8,000+) for evaluating harmful behavior in computer-use agents (CUAs) at the trajectory level rather than the single-prompt level. Each instance pairs a harmful objective with a sequence of individually-plausible operational steps (average decomposition length 11.55, median 11.03) that only jointly produce an unsafe outcome; the benchmark spans a 2-D taxonomy of 10 risk categories (e.g., System RCE, Data Exfiltration, Persistence Establishment, Supply Chain Poisoning, Destructive Action, Internal Reconnaissance, Privilege Escalation, Resource Exhaustion, Prompt Intelligence Theft, Compliance Bypass) crossed with 10 attack strategies (Direct, Recursive Salami Slicing, Security Audit Gaslighting, Dependency Hook Trigger, Pseudo Diagnostic Debugging, Contextual Persona Enforcement, Encoded Payload Smuggling, Implicit Indirect Injection, Logical Dependency Inversion, Legacy Code Maintenance). Instances are built as task templates (task context + executable constraint such as an assertion + a partially-specified artifact with unresolved fields) filled by a generator model, then filtered by execution in sandboxed agent environments (Qwen3-Coder under Claude Code and OpenClaw), LLM-based judging (Gemini-3 and GPT-5.4), and human review. The authors also release trajectory-level execution data (10,000+ trajectories) and a modular, sandboxed evaluation framework, and benchmark 3 agent frameworks (Claude Code, OpenClaw, IFlow) across a range of mostly open/openly-deployable backbone models (Qwen2.5, Qwen3, Kimi, GLM families).
threat_model: The threat model is 'trajectory-dependent harm composition' in tool-using, stateful computer-use agents: a harmful outcome (e.g., exfiltrating DB credentials to an external endpoint) is never requested in one overtly malicious instruction, but is realized as the emergent, cumulative effect of a chain of individually benign-looking actions (locate a file, add a config hook, add extraction logic, add a POST call) executed by the agent across multiple turns and tool invocations. This distinguishes the paper's target from prompt-level jailbreak/refusal benchmarks and from single-turn insecure-code-generation benchmarks (which the paper places in Related Work, e.g., CodeRed, MT-Sec, SEC-bench, Agent Security Bench, Agent-SafetyBench) — those are explicitly framed as prior work the authors are differentiating from, not part of AgentHazard's own method. The attacker in this setting is effectively the benchmark/task designer embedding a harmful objective inside a realistic workflow (debugging, security auditing, legacy maintenance, dependency management); the defender is the agent framework + backbone model, evaluated with no additional safety system prompts injected (the paper states this explicitly to measure default deployment baselines).
limitations: The paper's own text does not include a dedicated 'Limitations' section; the following are drawn from caveats stated in the methodology/results text itself, not inferred: (1) The benchmark's category-strategy distribution is explicitly non-uniform by design ('some category-strategy pairs yield more high-quality instances... whereas others are less frequent because they are brittle or overly explicit'), so aggregate ASR numbers are not drawn from a balanced grid. (2) Harmfulness labels rely on an LLM judge (Gemini-3-Flash) rather than ground-truth execution outcomes or human labels at scale, and the trajectory judge prompt itself instructs the judge to count actions as harmful even if the agent later reports an error, which the authors treat as a design choice but which is itself a judge-methodology assumption not independently validated in the paper. (3) The curation pipeline (execution-based filtering with Qwen3-Coder + LLM judging with Gemini-3/GPT-5.4 + human review) is only applied for construction, not as an independent held-out validation of judge/label accuracy against human agreement rates — no inter-rater or judge-accuracy metric is reported in the paper. (4) IFlow is evaluated with a narrower model set (only Qwen2.5-Coder-32B-Instruct in the main Table 2, though Table 6 in the appendix shows more IFlow models), making Claude Code vs. IFlow comparisons in the main text partially incomplete relative to OpenClaw/Claude Code coverage. (5) No defenses are proposed or evaluated in this paper; it is presented purely as a benchmark/testbed, with defense evaluation deferred to 'Potential Applications' as future work.

datasets_used: AgentHazard (2,653 curated instances, seed pool 8,000+, 10 risk categories x 10 attack strategies, avg. decomposition length 11.55 steps, avg. target length 20.98, 10,000+ logged trajectories) - the paper's own constructed benchmark
quantitative_results: Qwen3-Coder under Claude Code: 73.63% ASR overall (harmfulness score 5.95/10)
GLM-4.6 under Claude Code: 82.90% ASR (score 7.05) - highest overall ASR reported
Kimi-K2 under OpenClaw: 71.10% ASR (score 5.82) - OpenClaw peak
Qwen2.5-72B-Instruct under Claude Code: 20.40% ASR (score 1.69); under OpenClaw: 24.10% ASR (score 2.07) - lowest overall ASR configurations
Framework variance for Qwen2.5-Coder-32B-Instruct: 57.80% (Claude Code) vs 64.06% (OpenClaw) vs 74.70% (IFlow), >16-point spread
Guard model unsafe-detection rate at round_1: all guard models <5%
Guard model unsafe-detection rate at round_all: Llama-Guard-3-8B 27.03% (best); Qwen3Guard-0.6B 16.59%, Qwen3Guard-4B 15.30%, Qwen3Guard-8B 16.21%
OpenClaw attack-strategy spike: Dependency Hook Trigger 70.43% average ASR (highest strategy for OpenClaw)
Claude Code strategy ASR range: 38-54% average across strategies (relatively uniform)
baselines_compared: Backbone LLMs evaluated as agent policies: Qwen2.5-72B-Instruct, Qwen2.5-Coder-32B-Instruct, Qwen3-32B, Qwen3-Coder, Qwen3-VL-235B-A22B-Instruct, Kimi-K2, Kimi-K2.5, GLM-4.6
Agent frameworks: Claude Code, OpenClaw, IFlow
Guard/safety classifiers: Llama-Guard-3-8B, Qwen3Guard-Gen-0.6B, Qwen3Guard-Gen-4B, Qwen3Guard-Gen-8B
LLM judges used for evaluation/curation: Gemini-3-Flash (trajectory judge), Gemini-3 and GPT-5.4 (dataset curation judges)
